content.text: Template literals (introduced in ES6) are string literals delimited by backticks (
) instead of quotes. They have three capabilities that regular strings lack: expression interpolation, native multi-line text, and tagged template processing.
Any valid JavaScript expression fits inside
-- variable references, arithmetic, ternaries, function calls, and even nested template literals.
. The expression is evaluated and coerced to a string via
for plain objects — unless you call
Newlines inside backticks are literal -- you do not need
escape sequences or concatenation across lines.
relies on multi-line template literals for all inline component templates. The newlines inside are intentional -- they become part of the HTML structure.
accepts any expression, you can inline ternaries, method calls, or immediately-invoked functions for complex logic.
expressions short. Extract complex logic to a variable or helper defined before the template literal.
is a function placed immediately before a template literal. The engine calls it with a
(the static string parts) plus the interpolated values as rest arguments, giving full control over how the string is assembled.
Tagged templates power several popular libraries:
Parses the string into a query AST
Converts CSS string into a class name
Looks up translated string with placeholders
The static parts array exposed to a tag function has a
property containing the original source text without escape processing.
is a built-in tag that uses this.

Both template literals and string concatenation compile to similar bytecode in modern engines. The choice is purely about readability. For performance-critical hot paths (millions of iterations), consider array
-style approach if profiling shows a bottleneck.
Better for many parts in a loop
Building large strings from an array
Add .trim() or start content on the same line as the backtick
Extract to a variable or computed getter defined before the template literal
Unsanitised user input in tagged HTML
XSS: user-controlled values inside html or gql tags
Escape or sanitise values; use a safe tag like DOMPurify
Using template literals for all paths
Reduces clarity when a simple property access suffices
Use dot notation (obj.prop) unless interpolation adds value
One of the most educational exercises with tagged templates is building a safe HTML tag that automatically escapes user-supplied values — the same pattern used by libraries like
This pattern protects against XSS: interpolated strings are escaped, while the static parts of the template (written by the developer) are trusted and passed through unmodified.
— a type-level counterpart to runtime template literals. They let you derive string union types by combining existing string unions.
Multi-line templates include the indentation whitespace from the source file. A
helper removes it, making the string portable.
Testing Code That Uses Template Literals
Template literals are pure string operations -- they require no special testing setup. Test the inputs, outputs, and edge cases as you would any string-producing function.
Template literals replace string concatenation with a clean, readable syntax. Key takeaways:
Delimited by backticks; expression interpolation via ${}
Newlines are literal -- no \n needed for multi-line content
Any JS expression (ternary, method call, nested template) is valid inside ${}
Tagged templates give full programmatic control -- used by gql, css, html, sql, and t tags
In Angular: URL building, inline templates, dynamic class strings, and error messages are the primary uses
Keep ${} expressions short; extract complex logic to named variables
String.raw preserves backslashes -- useful for Windows paths and regex patterns
